documents.title: Radio Discipline Fundamentals
documents.content: Keep transmissions short, structured, and purposeful. Use agreed call signs and authentication words. Confirm critical details by read-back. Avoid unnecessary chatter. Schedule radio checks and fallback windows so silence does not create confusion.

documents.title: Standard Message Format
documents.content: Use concise sequence: who you are calling, who you are, priority, location, request, and acknowledgment needed. Repeat coordinates carefully. For emergency traffic, mark as immediate and keep channel clear until resolved.

documents.title: Low-Tech Signal Plans
documents.content: Prepare non-electronic alternatives: visual markers, whistle codes, timed checkpoints, and paper route cards. Predefine meaning of each signal and keep plans simple enough for stress conditions.

documents.title: Map and Grid Reference Basics
documents.content: Always orient map to terrain before route decisions. Use grid references consistently with local map standard. Confirm distance and elevation gain estimates. Mark water, shelter, and hazard points before movement.

documents.title: Compass Navigation Procedure
documents.content: Set bearing, identify terrain handrails, and move by short legs with regular backstops. Record pace count for terrain type. Reconfirm position at known landmarks instead of drifting long distances.

documents.title: Night Movement and Light Discipline
documents.content: Reduce silhouette and noise signatures. Use red light only when necessary and shield beams. Keep interval spacing and periodic accountability halts. Avoid crossing exposed terrain unless timing and concealment are favorable.

documents.title: Mesh Networking Basics
documents.content: A mesh network links many local nodes so data can hop from node to node without a central internet provider. Plan for short links with line-of-sight where possible, and design redundancy so one failed node does not isolate the whole group. Keep a paper map of node names, locations, power source, and responsible operator.

documents.title: Node Placement and Link Quality
documents.content: Prioritize high, stable mounting points with clear path to neighboring nodes. Trees and weather can change link quality over time, so test links in poor conditions, not only clear days. Use directional antennas for long point-to-point links and omnidirectional antennas for local neighborhood coverage.

documents.title: Mesh Network Operations and Security
documents.content: Assign maintainers for each node and enforce simple change control: document firmware versions, keys, and config backups before updates. Segment sensitive traffic from public bulletin traffic. Use strong admin credentials, encrypted management access, and routine key rotation. Keep local services such as community bulletin boards, map mirrors, and text-only knowledge libraries available even when upstream internet fails.

documents.title: Comms Resilience Planning
documents.content: Plan layered comms: primary radio, secondary frequency, tertiary runner/signals, and local mesh for non-urgent coordination. Maintain spare power budget and spare antennas/connectors. Drill failure scenarios monthly to keep procedures automatic.
